these markers of inflammation are associated with an increased risk of disease exacerbations
and death [4–6,16,17].
With regard to WBC count and its subtypes, many reports have shown the prognostic value
of an elevated WBC count and neutrophilia in the systemic circulation in patients with COPD.
In a large Danish COPD population study, an elevated WBC count in combination with ele-
vated levels of CRP and fibrinogen was associated with a two- to four-fold increased risk of
major comorbidities such as ischemic heart disease, diabetes, lung cancer, and pneumonia [6].
In two retrospective cohort studies, WBC counts tended to increase in Global Initiative for
Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease (GOLD) stages 3 to 4 or GOLD grades C to D, as compared
